coredata.dc:title: Solid state photoluminescence thermoplastic starch film containing graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Javanbakht, Siamak
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent polymer films, a matrix of thermoplastic starch (TPS) based bio-polymer and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were fabricated by a casting method. The GQDs provide solid state fluorescent properties to the prepared thermoplastic starch graphene quantum dots (TPS/GQD). The fluorescent, thermal, mechanical and optical properties of TPS/GQD were investigated. High optical transparency (88–91%) and well dispersion of GQDs (1–17wt%) in the polymeric matrix of TPS/GQD nanocomposite was observed. The maximum photoluminescence intensity of materials has been obtained at 50wt% of GQD content. These materials have great potential to use in flexible electronic displays, light emitting diodes (LED), GQD-LED packaging and other optoelectronics applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent polymer composites
Graphene quantum dots
Nanocomposites
Thermoplastic starch
Photoluminescence
Luminescent polymer composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 938645702) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial supports for this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra for the neat TPS, GQD, TPS/GQD nanocomposites with 9wt%, 28.5wt% and 50wt% of GQD content.
TEM images of the TPS/GQD composites containing (a) 5wt%, (b) 17wt%, (c) 28.5wt% and (d) 50wt% GQDs.
SEM images of the (a) GQDs, (b) TPS and TPS/GQD composites containing (c) 5wt%, (d) 17wt%, (e) 37.5wt% and (f) 50wt% GQDs.
The UV/vis spectra of the neat TPS films and TPS/GQD composites containing different GQD concentrations.
Optical transparency (measured at 365nm for (a) and 460nm for (b)) vs GQD concentration in the TPS/GQD composite films.
TGA profiles for the TPS and TPS/GQD composite films in air (a) and DSC thermograms for the neat TPS and TPS/GQD composite films with different filler content (b).
Tensile strength (a) and Elongation at break (b) for the neat TPS and TPS/GQD composite films with different filler content.

Photoluminescence spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs and UV–vis absorption of the GQDs; The photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs under 365nm UV light (a); DLS spectrum of GQDs (b).

(a) Photograph of the TPS and TPS/GQD films containing 9wt%, 17wt%, 37.5wt%, 44wt% and 50wt% GQDs under UV lamp (365nm wavelength). Film diameter is 3cm and thickness is ∼10μm. (b) Photograph showing in a dark room without UV illumination.
Photoluminescence spectra of TPS and TPS/GQD composite films(a). and the integrated areas of the photoluminescence spectra of the TPS/GQD films vs the corresponding GQD concentrations(b).
Thermal properties of the TPS/GQD composites.

head.ce:title: Solid state photoluminescence thermoplastic starch film containing graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent polymer films, a matrix of thermoplastic starch (TPS) based bio-polymer and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were fabricated by a casting method. The GQDs provide solid state fluorescent properties to the prepared thermoplastic starch graphene quantum dots (TPS/GQD). The fluorescent, thermal, mechanical and optical properties of TPS/GQD were investigated. High optical transparency (88–91%) and well dispersion of GQDs (1–17wt%) in the polymeric matrix of TPS/GQD nanocomposite was observed. The maximum photoluminescence intensity of materials has been obtained at 50wt% of GQD content. These materials have great potential to use in flexible electronic displays, light emitting diodes (LED), GQD-LED packaging and other optoelectronics applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Quantum Dots (QD) have various applications in solar cells, (Kamat, 2008) light emitting diode, (Coe, Woo, Bawendi, & Bulović, 2002; Colvin, Schlamp, & Alivisatos, 1994) bioimaging (Michalet et al., 2005), electronic displays (Wood et al., 2009), LEDs packaging (Xie, Hu, & Luo, 2016) and other optoelectronic devices, they become an interesting research field for scientists, however, the high price of inorganic Quantum dots (Sanderson, 2009), limit their industrial applications. Recently the new class of QDs, such as Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) and GQDs have been emerged (Huang et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2006) with the following advantages: low price, non-toxicity, high solubility, stable photoluminescence and high surface grafting ability introduce GQDs as an irreplaceable candidate for Indium tin oxide (Bonaccorso, Sun, Hasan, & Ferrari, 2010) for fabrication of optoelectronic devices (Sekiya, Uemura, Murakami, & Haino, 2014), such as Organic solar cells (Miao et al., 2012), organic light emitting diode (Han et al., 2012) and photovoltaic devices (Paulo, Palomares, & Martinez-Ferrero, 2016). Recent researches turned out that the synthesis of GQDs from Citric acid, make its industrial production possible from the cheap and renewable materials, while other methods (Guo, Wang, Yu, Chen, & Chen, 2012; Huang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2013; Li, Zhang, Kulinich, Liu, & Zeng, 2014; Li et al., 2011; Pan, Zhang, Li, & Wu, 2010; Sun et al., 2006; Zhang, Zhang, Chen, & Qu, 2012) including laser ablation (Sun et al., 2006), electron beam lithography (Li et al., 2013) or electro chemical (Li et al., 2011) are not practical for industrial scale. It is believed that the synthesis of GQDs from Citric acid with economical and industrial purpose could be developed in the near future. There are various reports about the synthesis of GQDs through cheap and available materials including ascorbic acid (Huang et al., 2014), gelatin (Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013) and coal (Dong et al., 2012) as a cost effective method in high scale production.

In order to apply the GQDs in the optoelectronic and photonic field on actual devices, (Farmer & Patten, 2001; Li, Zhang et al., 2014; Mao et al., 2013; Pang, Shen, Tetz, & Fainman, 2005; Tomczak, Jańczewski, Han, & Vancso, 2009) they should integrate in a transparent polymer matrix to provide enhanced mechanical and chemical stability. It is important to introduce a polymer with low aggregation index to provide high interactions with QDs in order to enhance the emission properties required for the production of appropriate thin films. Since GQDs have high solubility in water, choosing a suitable polymer for casting process has high importance. Due to starch’s ability in conversion to thermoplastic materials, along with low price, availability, biodegradability, suitable transparency in the thermoplastic form, water solubility, casting and processing ability, make starch a preferred candidate for polymeric matrix (Janssen & Moscicki, 2009; Röper & Koch, 1990; Stepto, 2003). In the recent studies about converting processes of starch to thermoplastic polymers, the production of new plasticizers based on starch and their properties is in great concerns (Da Róz, Carvalho, Gandini, & Curvelo, 2006; Heydarifard, Pan, Xiao, Nazhad, & Shipin, 2017; Peres, Pires, & Oréfice, 2016). One of the main plasticizers for thermoplastic starch is glycerol (Lourdin, Bizot, & Colonna, 1997; Poutanen & Forssell, 1996). However, there are other compounds including ethylene glycol, (Lourdin, Coignard, Bizot, & Colonna, 1997; Poutanen & Forssell, 1996) urea, (Shogren, Swanson, & Thompson, 1992) sorbitol and supplementary sugars (Poutanen & Forssell, 1996). The amount and the chemistry of plasticizer could affect the properties of processed starch, through one of the following pathways, (I): Controlling the degradation and polymerization, (II): affecting the final properties of materials such as glass transition temperature and standard temperature. The high amount of plasticizers in TPS could separate the phases and also increase the sensitivity to humid (Lourdin, Coignard et al., 1997).

Meanwhile, the obtained GQDs from Citric acid have abundant polar groups on its edges and basal plane; provide the possibility to use the prepared GQDs in polymeric composite without any surface modification. Based on the above explanation, GQDs has been selected as filler for nanobiocomposite based thermoplastic starch film. It is feasible to use GQDs in the process of modification of starch into thermoplastic films in the presence of plasticizer and water before casting step. Recently some papers were published related to the nanocomposite with starch containing GO (Ferreira & Andrade, 2015; Wu, Bäckström, & Hakkarainen, 2017) however, there is not any report about TPS containing GQDs. The TPS/GQDs films were fabricated and the related fluorescence properties were investigated.
All the starting materials including corn starch, citric acid, glycerol and all other used materials were purchased from Merck and used without further purification.

GQDs were prepared according to the previously reported method (Dong et al., 2012). In a typical synthesis, 2 gr of Citric acid monohydrate was put into a 5ml beaker and heated up to 200°C using a heating mantle. After 30min the obtained orange liquid of prepared GQDs was added dropwise into 100ml of 10mg/ml NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. Then it has been neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH to produce the 2% stock solution of GQDs.

The thermoplastic starch and the subsequent nanocomposites was obtained using casting method described by Angellier, Molina-Boisseau, Dole, and Dufresne (2006); with some modifications. 2 gr of crude native corn starch and 1.08 gr of glycerol was added to different volume of prepared GQDs stock solution (ranging from 1ml of stock solution to obtain composite films with 1wt% concentration to 100ml of stock solution to afford 50wt% concentration) and adjust the volume of the mixture to 100ml by distilled water. Complete gelatinization occurs at 80°C with continues stirring about 30min. Then the materials were spread into polystyrene Petri dish and dried in an oven at 50°C for one day.

The IR spectrums were obtained on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) in the range of 4000–400cm−1 at a resolution of 0.5cm−1 as KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed at room temperature by Siemens diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at 35kV in the scan range of 2θ from 5 to 70°. X’Pert High Score Plus was used to refine the single-crystal parameters to the measured powder diffraction profile. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) was conducted by LEO 906E transmission electron microscope operating at 100kV. Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) were obtained with a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope operating at 5kV. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements was obtained with a DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). UV–vis spectroscopy was carried out on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 UV–vis absorption spectrometer at room temperature. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis of the materials was performed using DSC (Linseis-L63/45, Germany) at 10°C/min heating rate in the temperature range of 25–350°C. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on TGA instrument (Linseis TGA 1750) at a heating rate of 10°C/min, from 25°C to 600°C. DSC and TGA were analyzed under air atmosphere.

The solid states photoluminescence (PL) spectra were received by a spectrofluorometric (FP-6200, Jasco, Japan) and the aqueous solutions of GQDs were recorded by a spectrofluorometric (LS45, PerkinElmer). The film quantum yield (QY) was calculated by the previous established method (Tseng, Choi, Davidson, & Holloway, 2010).

Hand-held micrometer (Dial Thickness Gauge 7301, Mitutoyo Corporation, Kanagawa, Japan) was used to measure the thickness of the film samples. For each film thickness, the average values of five random areas were measured. Each film was cut into molding strips and the elongation at break and tensile strength of the TPS/GQD composite films were measured according to the standard test method of ASTM D 882-88 using Instron Universal Testing Machine (SANTAM, Model STM1, Iran). For each sample, the average value of six measurements was reported (Rakhshaei & Namazi, 2017).

Fig. 1 shows the FT-IR spectrum of TPS, GQDs and TPS/GQD composites in different concentrations. The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized composite was similar to the spectrum of pure TPS and GQDs, and the intensity of GQDs peaks (∼1589cm−1) increased as the concentration of GQDs increased.

The GQD dispersion in the thin TPS/GQD composite films was shown in the typical TEM images (Fig. 2 ). A homogeneous GQD dispersion in the polymer matrix can be seen in the images at lower concentration of GQDs. The low concentration of GQD as filler in the polymeric matrix (5wt%) almost was not aggregated (Fig. 2a). Probably due to the aggregation of GQD at 17wt% the particle sizes appeared to be larger (Fig. 2b). An increase in the GQD loading percentage up to 17–28.5wt%, could increase the particle aggregation with normal cluster size under 100nm with slow slope. A considerable agglomeration of nanoparticle was detected at concentrations of GQD around 28.5wt% and above, with a creation of slack agglomerates making dimensions >300nm (Fig. 2d) (Kovalchuk, Huang, Xiang, Martí, & Tour, 2015). The GQDs exhibit well dispersibility in the TPS matrix without any extra surface treatment of these nanoparticles. This is a remarkable benefit of GQDs compare to inorganic QDs that usually need for surface treatment to prevent agglomeration (Farmer & Patten, 2001; Li, Zhang et al., 2014).

Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM images of GQDs obtained from citric acid and TPS respectively, GQDs poses a uniform circular shape (4–6nm), TPS similar to amorphous compounds has a soft and homogeneous surface. The SEM images of 3c–3f exhibited the distribution of GQDs nanoparticles in TPS/GQD nanocomposite films.

The SEM images indicated that in the nanocomposites with low concentration of GQDs nanoparticles, the homogeneous dispensation of GQDs was spotted while as the concentration of GODs nanoparticles increased, an agglomeration occurred that has been shown in SEM images. Fig. 3c, belong to the composite with low loading percent of GQDs (5wt%) that as it has been shown, there is not any agglomeration in a polymeric matrix. But as the concentration of GQDs increased to 17wt%, the size of nanoparticles increased due to the agglomeration (Fig. 2d). In the high concentration of GQDs (˃17wt%), the uniform and soft surface of TPS/GQD films destroyed, which is probably originated from steric effect of converting process of starch to thermoplastic materials, in which the high concentration of nanoparticles affects the discipline of polymeric chains and as a result, the morphology of the film surface changed. In the concentration of 37.5wt%, agglomeration on the composite film surface was detected (Fig. 3e), even more agglomeration in the 50wt% concentration was detected on composite film surface led to complete disintegration of the uniform morphology of TPS/GQD nanocomposite film (Fig. 3f). The GQDs originated from citric acid have great advantages compare to inorganic QDs. due to the amphiphilic nature and existence of polar groups, it is possible to uniformly distribute the GQDs in the polymeric matrix of starch with less agglomeration without any surface modification, while inorganic QDs require surface modification to prevent agglomeration.

UV/vis spectroscopy provides useful information about the optical properties and the structure of the composite film. Fig. 4 Shows the UV/vis spectrum of neat TPS and TPS/GQD with an approximate thickness of 10μm. As Fig. 4 shows, with increasing the GQDs concentration, a steady red shift and a decrease in transparency around 365nm were observed, that is mainly attributed to GQDs aggregation that has absorption wavelength at 365nm (Fig. 5 a). The mentioned aggregation was also observed in SEM images. Due to narrow absorption of GQD nanoparticles in the 365nm, a drastic reduction in the transparency was observed which is more distinguished in the primary wavelengths, but for the 460nm which is the maximum emission wavelength of GQDs, no obvious loss of transparency was detected (Fig. 5b) (Yadollahi, Namazi, & Barkhordari, 2014).

Fig. 6 a shows the diagram of TGA analysis for the synthesized compounds. The TPS and TPS/GQD films lose 4–8% of water in the temperature range of 50–150°C. According to the previous reports, usually the GQDs were synthesized through pyrolysis at 200°C. When the temperature reaches to 200°C, GQDs experience another pyrolysis process in the polymeric matrix. Leading to the observation of a carbonization and condensation reaction among carboxylic acid of GQDs and hydroxyl groups of starch by losing a water molecule, removing a water molecule resulted in the weight loss. According to the TGA data, the presence of GQDs in the polymeric matrix, changed the maximum weight loss from ∼320°C for pure TPS to ∼280°C for composite. The weight loss increased with increasing the GQDs concentration, which is probably due to the created disorders and solidarity that altered the mechanism of the composite’s degradation. Since the exact mechanism is not fully understood, more study is required to clarify it.

The neat TPS destroyed around 600°C, but for the prepared composite, there are still up to ∼10% weight of composite remained in the container after burning process which is probably related to the reduction of GQDs by polymer through thermal reduction process (Ferreira & Andrade, 2015).

The DSC thermograms of TPS and TPS/GQD nanocomposites are depicted in Fig. 6b. The film sample was cut into small pieces with 10mg weight and was placed in a sample pan. An empty high volume pan was used as a reference. Increasing the concentration of GQDs from 1 to 50wt%, decrease the polymer melting temperature (Tm) from 286°C (for the neat TPS) to 204°C (for the TPS/GQD composites). This decrease in Tm can be attributed to the decrease in the molar fraction of the polymer. The melting enthalpy (ΔHm) of the composites indicates a gentle increase and subsequently a decrease, by increasing the GQD concentration (Table 1 ). The second peak in DSC thermograms related to the thermal reduction of GQD by the polymer with few variations. The re-crystallization peak has been observed around 140–160°C when the concentration of GQDs is more than 33wt% (Chung & Lim, 2004). The broad strong hydroxyl band was observed between 3000 and 3500cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1), that is possibly originated from H-bonding, between the free and H-bonding alcohols of the polymer matrix and nanoparticle filler. Nevertheless, due to the small difference in the strength of hydroxyl band for the TPS/GQD composites, it is almost impossible to get a clear conclusion. Which is in good coincidence with previous reports (Ferreira & Andrade, 2015; Kovalchuk et al., 2015). It seems that probably these difference in the polymer Tm and ΔHm were created from a combination of various factors, such as; structural disorder, steric effects and hydrogen bonding resulted by the intercalates of GQDs. Furthermore, apparently with agglomeration of nanoparticle in the polymeric matrix at a high concentration of GQD, the Tm and ΔHm were decreased.

The tensile strength of the neat TPS and TPS/GQD was measured. Neat TPS showed a tensile strength of 19.2MPa (Fig. 7 a). The incorporation of GQD to 17wt% content in the polymeric matrix increase the tensile strength, that is due to the well dispersion of GQDs nanoparticle inside the polymeric matrix. According to the obtained results, the TPS/GQDs had sufficient strength to absorb the applied force. However, with increasing the GQDs content from 17 to 50wt% the strength of composite decreased, due to the aggregation of the nanoparticle. The flexibility of the material was specified using the elongation at break index. The TPS/GQD composite films showed a slight variation in the elongation at break point (Fig. 7b), while neat TPS had more flexibility than the TPS/GQD composite films. The achieved data approved the suitable flexibility of the TPS/GQDs composite films.

A wide peak (002) around 2θ=26.98° in the XRD patterns of GQDs was detected (Fig. 8 a), it seems it should be a good evidence for carbonizing of Citric acid to yield graphene structures (Li, Lau, Tang, Ji, & Yang, 2014). Further characterizing of GQD that is shown in Fig. 8 are in good consistency to previous reports for the synthesis of GQD from Citric acid (Dong et al., 2012). Fig. 8b and d respectively exhibited the photoluminescence, UV/vis spectrum and digital photographs of the luminescence emission of GQDs under UV lamp irradiation at 365nm. Incorporation of GQDs in the polymeric thermoplastic starch matrixes create the photoluminescence properties. Digital photographs of prepared films with a diameter of 3cm and 10μm thickness were located on the glass support under UV lamp was depicted in Fig. 8. Because of photoluminescence properties of the prepared films, by turning the UV lamp on, the films become more bright. For the neat TPS films, no emission was spotted, while by increasing the concentration of loaded GQD, the intensity of emitted light was increased. (Fig. 9 a) The color of the emitted light was white inclined to pale blue. There was no emission in the absences of excitement photons. (Fig. 9b).

As shown in Fig. 10 a, the solid-state photoluminescence spectrum of TPS/GQD nanocomposite films was used to study the quantitative properties of the synthesized composites. Fig. 10b exhibited the integral of photoluminescence spectrum versus the GQDs concentration. By comparing the results, it could be concluded that the photoluminescence spectrum of TPS/GQD compare to GQDs spectrum in water become wider and a splitting pattern in the spectrum was observed, that is mainly due to creation and amplification of phenomenon’s like intersystem transitions, internal and external transformations and self-absorbing as a result of aggregation of GQDs nanoparticles. The intensity of photoluminescence of composites was dependent on the concentration of loaded GQDs nanoparticles and by increasing the concentration of GQDs from 1 to 17wt% the intensity was increased and for concentration among 17–28.5wt% it was reduced and for concentration of 28.5–50wt%, it was increased. The maximum intensity was observed for 50wt.%. This variation in intensity is probably attributed to aggregation of GQDs nanoparticles in the polymeric matrix. By increasing the concentration of GQDs nanoparticles to 17wt% the intensity of photoluminescence amplified, due to an increase in the photoluminescence spaces in the prepared films. But at the same time by addition of more GQDs, the various factors such as inner filter effect, photoluminescence reabsorption, partial quenching as a result of creation of different hunk particles created from aggregation, leaded to a decrease in photoluminescence intensity of TPS/GQD composite, which is detectable in the concentration range of 17–28.5wt%. With increasing the GQDs content from 28.5 to 50wt% a cluster with higher aggregation of GQDs nanoparticles in dimension of ∼300nm was observed. However, simultaneously the regions with lower aggregation at the edges of the same clusters have been created. It seems that they changed the surface morphology, and probably increase surface area for possible excitation and emission transition and probably they cause an increase in the photoluminescence properties of TPS/GQD nanocomposite (Kovalchuk et al., 2015). The maximum quantum yield for TPS/GQD composite with concentration of 50wt% of GQDs nanoparticles was 8.20±0.05% that is in agreement with previously reported quantum yields from GQDs in solution (Dong et al., 2012).

Since QDs emission properties are depended on the particle size, it seems that the wide emission spectrum of TPS/GQD composite film was originated from GQD nanoparticles with different size which is detectable in DLS analysis (Fig. 8c). Because these materials support a wide emission spectrum of visible light, the synthesized TPS/GQD has the potential to be used in the white light emitting LED as a luminophore. Usually to obtain a white light in LEDs, a combination of different light sources such as blue and yellow light emitting QDs was used. GQDs composites could be commercialized for white LEDs as a blend with other QDs to improve the white light emission. However, the TPS/GQD composite itself is able to be used in this field.

In summary, the GQDs obtained from Citric acid have been successfully incorporated in TPS film, by casting method from environmentally friendly aqueous solutions with water as the solvent. The GQDs originated from Citric acid compared to inorganic QDs are capable of distributing uniformly in the polymeric matrix of starch with less agglomeration without any surface requirement. This is an important advantage because inorganic QDs require surface modification to prevent from agglomeration. The TPS/GQD composites have luminescence properties with intensity dependence to the concentration of GQD content; the photoluminescence reached its maximum at 50wt% of loading. The TPS/GQD nanocomposites exhibited broad photoluminescence emission spectra, able to support most of the visible range, opening up the possibility of using these materials in white light LEDs as luminophores. Due to Its low price, low production cost, availability, biodegradability, suitable for large-scale industrial applications, water solubility, and processing ability, make the Citric acid GQDs as a preferred candidate to be used as a cost-effective and eco-friendly system compares to inorganic quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 938645702) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial supports for this research.
